prompt: Name one commonly-praised idea, trend, or practice in modern knowledge work that you think is overrated, and explain why.
response:
“Deep-work marathons” – the cult of blocking off four, six, or even eight uninterrupted hours every day to do cognitively demanding work.

Why it’s overrated:

1. Most knowledge jobs are interrupt-driven systems, not craft benches.  
   Your calendar is coupled to other people’s: sales calls, legal reviews, customer fires, the CFO’s model that breaks at noon. Pretending those couplings don’t exist doesn’t make them disappear; it just turns the deep-work block into a daily guilt trip when it inevitably fractures.

2. Cognitive stamina is a sharply tailed resource.  
   For the median person, the marginal minute after ~90–120 minutes of genuine focus is no longer “deep”; it’s make-work that feels virtuous but produces negative returns (more typos, over-engineering, rabbit holes). The extra hours are more about signaling asceticism than shipping better answers.

3. It crowds out the other half of creativity: loose, weak-tie input.  
   The same Slack messages, hallway questions, and “pointless” meetings people flee are also where they overhear the constraint that makes their elegant model useless, or the analogy from another team that solves the problem. Deep-work fundamentalism starves the stochastic inputs that recombination requires.

4. It scales badly to senior contribution.  
   Junior roles reward individual throughput; senior roles reward multiplier decisions—one 15-minute conversation that saves six people a week. Optimizing for forehead-on-desk time actively trains you to hoard the work instead of distributing it.

5. It becomes a performative identity (“I’m a deep worker, therefore I’m serious”).  
   That identity discourages measurement. When the block is sacred, you don’t A/B whether the deliverable is actually better; you just feel righteous. It’s the knowledge-work version of busy-ness.

Shorter, protected focus sprints (60–90 min, 1–3× per day) plus aggressive delegation and asynchronous tools routinely outperform the monastery schedule in real-world, multi-player organizations.
